title: Food Coloring on Cotton
stainChemistry: Food coloring is concentrated azo / triphenylmethane / natural food dyes in water + glycerin carrier. Red 40, Blue 1, Yellow 5 (Tartrazine) are the FDA-approved synthetic azo / triphenylmethane dyes that bond to cotton through the same grip as deliberate dye application. Fresh food coloring rinses easily; set food coloring (through a hot dryer) is often permanent. Cotton tolerates the standard dye-stain toolkit (NSD, IPA, OxiClean, 3% H₂O₂) but these work BEFORE heat-setting. Red 40 is the hardest variant because it's concentrated.
whyThisWorks: Fresh food coloring + cold water + immediate cycle = 70–90% removal. Set food coloring + home chemistry plateaus. OxiClean soak is the home workhorse for whites. Pro-side reducing bleaches (Rit Color Remover / sodium hydrosulfite) strip harder on whites. Speed + escalation discipline matter more than chemistry choice.
defaultAssumption: Context is usually baking / Easter eggs / kid crafts / homemade playdough. Fresh spill = high-confidence DIY. Discovered-after-dryer spill = often pro or accept-as-is.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Cold water, white cotton terry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cold flush from the BACK. Fresh food coloring rinses rapidly. Cold only — heat sets dye.

spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD or Dawn in cold water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply NSD or Dawn, work with a soft brush. Rinse cool. Repeat if color transfers to cloth.
Submerge the stained area in OxiClean + warm water. Soak 1–4 hours, overnight for stubborn color. Oxidizes the dye color on whites and most pastels.
For residual color on WHITE cotton after OxiClean: 3% H₂O₂. Skip on dark cotton.
White cotton only. If color persists, Rit Color Remover uses sodium hydrosulfite to strip residual dye. Ventilate — sulfur smell. Do NOT use on colored clothes — strips fabric dye too.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Sodium hydrosulfite = respiratory irritant. Ventilate. Gloves. Never mix with chlorine bleach.

spottingProtocol.technique: Launder cold, do not hot-dry with residual color
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cold wash per care label. Air-dry until stain is fully gone. Any heat on remaining food coloring = permanent.

steps: 1. Cold flush from the back — speed matters.
5. Rit Color Remover for stubborn dye (whites only, sulfur smell).
6. Cold wash + air-dry until stain is fully gone.
products: Rit Color Remover (whites only, nuclear option)
warnings: Rit Color Remover strips ALL dye — whites only

homeSolutions.instruction: Food coloring is concentrated dye. Caught fresh = easy. Caught after a hot dryer cycle = often permanent. Drop what you're doing and get cold water on it. Cold only — heat sets.
Hold the BACK of the stained fabric under cold running water for a minute. The water-soluble dye rinses out while it's still mobile.
Work a drop of Dawn into the spot with a fingertip. Rinse cool. Repeat 2–3 cycles for concentrated colors (Red 40 is the toughest).
Fill a basin with warm water + 1 scoop OxiClean per gallon. Submerge the garment. Soak 1–4 hours — overnight for heavy stains. OxiClean's oxygen bleach oxidizes the food dye's color.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix OxiClean with chlorine bleach, ammonia, or hydrogen peroxide.

homeSolutions.instruction: For residual color on white cotton: dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide on the spot. Skip on dark cotton. Rinse OxiClean out first — one oxidizer at a time.

homeSolutions.agent: COLD Wash, Air-Dry Until Fully Clear
homeSolutions.instruction: Cold wash per care label if stain is still visible. AIR-DRY — do not run through a dryer until the stain is fully gone. Heat permanently sets food coloring.

If food coloring has been hot-dried, take to a dry cleaner. For whites specifically, Rit Color Remover is a home nuclear option (sodium hydrosulfite, sulfur smell — ventilate). Don't use on colored clothes — strips the fabric's own dye.

steps: 1. SPEED MATTERS — every minute of dry contact matters.
2. Cold water flush from the BACK.
5. 3% peroxide on residual (whites only, flush OxiClean first).
6. COLD wash + AIR-DRY until stain is fully gone.
7. Escalate if heat-set. Rit Color Remover is the home nuclear option (whites).
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if food coloring has been hot-dried — often permanent at that point.
warnings: HEAT PERMANENTLY SETS food coloring — never hot-dry visible dye
Rit Color Remover strips ALL dye — whites only
Never layer OxiClean + peroxide + chlorine bleach
Red 40 is the hardest variant — budget extra cycles

neverDo: Never hot-wash or dry visible food coloring — permanent
Never use Rit Color Remover on colored cotton
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates OxiClean, 3% H₂O₂, Rit Color Remover, cold wash
Dyed cotton: Rit Color Remover strips fabric dye — whites only

materialWarnings: Rit Color Remover = whites only (strips all dye)
Speed matters more than chemistry choice

escalation.when: Food coloring has been hot-dried, OxiClean + peroxide haven't cleared it, or the fabric is colored and Rit Color Remover would strip its dye.
escalation.whatToTell: "Food coloring on cotton, color [red / blue / etc], age [fresh / hot-dried], home-treated with cold water + Dawn + OxiClean + [3% H₂O₂ whites]."

contextQuestions: What color — Red 40 is hardest; yellow and blue usually easier?
Has it been through a hot dryer?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Food coloring is the concentrated-dye stain where time + heat are the only real enemies. Home chemistry is strong; fresh on whites is usually winnable.
